articles.title: [RSS Club] A Sneak Preview of Upcoming Posts
articles.title_display: RSS Club Sneak Peek of Upcoming Blog Posts

articles.title: Madame Semver Will See You Now
articles.title_display: A Satirical Take on SemVer and Package Maintenance
articles.summary: This is a fictional, satirical piece about maintaining an open-source library under semantic versioning. It imagines recurring problems familiar to package maintainers: buggy pull requests, mistaken issue reports, namespace confusion, vulnerable scans, README drift, dependency forks, and the pressure to add back removed APIs for compatibility. The technical value is mostly in the recognizable maintainer pain points rather than any new process, benchmark, or concrete engineering guidance. Its main relevance is as a humorous reflection on the long-term costs of API stability and open-source stewardship.

articles.title: Debian must ship reproducible packages
articles.title_display: Debian Requires Reproducible Packages for Migration
articles.summary: Debian’s release team says new package migrations will now be blocked unless packages are reproducible, and existing testing packages that regress in reproducibility will also be held back. The change is enabled through the migration software and is credited to the Reproducible Builds project and the reproduce.debian.net infrastructure. The announcement also notes that autopkgtests are now run for binNMUs, not just source uploads, and warns that the new loong64 architecture has increased the CI queue because many packages had to be rebuilt across architectures. For maintainers, the practical impact is stricter release gating and more responsibility to fix reverse-dependency regressions before packages can migrate.

articles.title: Show HN: Building a web server in assembly to give my life (a lack of) meaning
articles.title_display: A Static HTTP Server Written Entirely in AArch64 Assembly
articles.summary:
This post describes ymawky, a static HTTP server implemented entirely in AArch64 assembly for macOS, using raw Darwin syscalls with no libc or external libraries. The author walks through the hardest parts of the project: manual HTTP parsing, request-method detection, path normalization and percent-decoding, header scanning, numeric parsing with overflow checks, and safe handling of `GET`, `HEAD`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, and `OPTIONS`.

The implementation emphasizes correctness and defensive behavior. It uses fork-per-request handling, temp-file writes for `PUT` to avoid partial overwrites, directory listing generation with both URL percent-encoding and HTML escaping, and filesystem protections such as docroot prefixing, `..` segment rejection, and `O_NOFOLLOW_ANY` to reduce traversal and symlink risks. It also adds timeout handling against slowloris-style attacks via `setitimer()` and `sigaction()`, plus a process cap using `proc_info()` to count active children.

The practical value is not performance claims but a detailed look at the complexity hidden inside “simple” web-server behavior when every byte and syscall must be handled manually.

articles.title: The left-wing case for AI
articles.summary: This essay argues that left-wing politics can support LLMs on pragmatic grounds: disability access, chronic-illness self-advocacy, class code-switching, and tutoring for students who lack privileged educational support. It frames AI as a tool that helps users interact with institutions in the language those institutions reward, and claims current frontier models may already reflect a broadly left-leaning bias. The piece is mostly ideological argumentation rather than technical analysis, but it does connect AI to real workflows in accessibility, medical research, and education. Its main practical takeaway is that the strongest pro-AI case on the left is not productivity, but access and power redistribution.

articles.title: Rust but Lisp
articles.title_display: Rust Syntax Reimagined as Lisp S-Expressions
articles.summary: This weekend project, rlisp, lets developers write Rust semantics in Lisp syntax and transpile the result to normal Rust source. The repo shows examples for structs, impls, enums, functions, closures, loops, modules, lifetimes, turbofish, if-let/while-let, unsafe blocks, and inline Rust escape hatches. It also includes macro support via compile-time s-expression transformers using quasiquote, unquote, and unquote-splicing, explicitly avoiding Rust’s proc_macro, token streaming, syn, and quote stack. The README emphasizes that rustc still performs type checking, borrow checking, and optimization; rlisp only handles syntax. The project ships with a quick-reference mapping between Lisp forms and Rust forms, installation via cargo install --path ., and Ariadne-based parse diagnostics. Practical significance is limited because this is a small experimental compiler frontend rather than a production tool, but it is a concrete demonstration of how Lisp-style macros and structural editing can be layered onto Rust.

articles.title: Show HN: I made a Clojure-like language in Go, boots in 7ms
articles.title_display: let-go: Clojure-like language in Go boots in 7ms
articles.summary:
let-go is a bytecode compiler and VM for a Clojure-like language implemented in Go, aimed at fast startup, small binaries, and practical interop. The repo claims a 10MB standalone binary with 7ms cold start and 14MB idle memory, and backs that with benchmark comparisons against Babashka, Joker, and Clojure on the JVM. On an Apple M1 Pro, it reports 4696/4921 passing assertions (95.4%) against the jank-lang Clojure compliance suite, with remaining gaps mostly around numeric edge cases, BigInt promotion, and a few unimplemented namespaces or forms.

The implementation includes broad Clojure surface-area support: macros, destructuring, protocols, records, multimethods, transducers, lazy seqs, core.async-like channels, HTTP, JSON, Transit, nREPL, and Babashka pods. It also supports AOT compilation to bytecode, standalone executables, and WASM web apps with terminal emulation. For Go integration, the project exposes Go functions, channels, and struct↔record roundtrips, so it can act as an embeddable scripting layer inside Go applications.

The most useful engineering detail is the benchmark table and the explicit list of behavioral differences from Clojure, which makes the project easier to evaluate for real adoption. This is most relevant for Go teams that want Lisp-style scripting, fast cold starts, or browser-capable CLJ-like tooling without the JVM.

articles.title: Using a Python 3 LSP server with Python 2 code works (more or less)
articles.title_display: Python 3 LSP Still Works on Python 2 Code
articles.summary: A GNU Emacs user reports that Python 3 LSP tools like pylsp and ruff can still be useful on legacy Python 2 code. In their experience, the servers did not produce a flood of false syntax errors, handled mixed tabs-and-spaces indentation surprisingly well, and supported basics like go-to-definition and find references. The main limitation noted is that pylsp still flags Python 2 print statements as invalid Python 3 syntax, which is often a reasonable cleanup target anyway. The post is a practical compatibility note for teams that still maintain Python 2 systems and want modern editor assistance without fully porting yet.

articles.title: FreeBSD: Local Privilege Escalation via Execve()
articles.title_display: FreeBSD execve() Bug Enables Local Privilege Escalation
articles.summary:
FreeBSD has disclosed CVE-2026-7270, a kernel vulnerability in execve(2) that can let an unprivileged local user escalate to root. The advisory says an operator-precedence bug in the kernel can trigger a buffer overflow, causing attacker-controlled data to overwrite adjacent execve argument buffers. All supported FreeBSD versions are affected.

The issue was corrected on 2026-04-29 across the supported stable and releng branches, including FreeBSD 15, 14.4, 14.3, and 13.5. FreeBSD provided multiple remediation paths: pkg(8) updates for 15.0 base-package installs, freebsd-update(8) for RELEASE systems, or source patching followed by a kernel rebuild and reboot. The advisory lists specific commit hashes for each fixed branch.

This is a high-priority system-level security fix because there is no workaround and the impact is full superuser compromise from local access. Administrators should patch immediately and reboot affected hosts.

articles.title: Bun's experimental Rust rewrite hits 99.8% test compatibility on Linux x64 glibc
articles.title_display: Bun’s Rust Rewrite Reaches 99.8% Test Compatibility
articles.summary:
Bun’s experimental Rust rewrite reportedly passes 99.8% of its pre-existing test suite on Linux x64 glibc. The post provides only a single compatibility number and no breakdown of what remains failing, which subsystems are covered, or how the rewrite performs on latency, memory use, startup time, or real workloads. There are also no benchmark comparisons against the current Bun runtime or competing JavaScript runtimes.

The signal is mainly that the rewrite is far enough along to preserve almost all existing behavior on one platform, which is useful for engineers tracking Bun’s architectural direction. But as a digest item, it is still an early status update rather than a technical deep dive, since it lacks implementation details, migration lessons, or evidence of broader production impact. It is relevant to JavaScript runtime and tooling engineers, but the practical takeaway is limited until more data is published.

articles.title: CPanel's Black Week: 3 New Vulnerabilities Patched After Attack on 44k Servers
articles.title_display: cPanel Patches Three More Flaws After 44,000-Server Attack
articles.summary: cPanel released a second emergency Technical Security Release on May 8, 2026, patching three vulnerabilities shortly after the CVE-2026-41940 auth-bypass incident that was reportedly used to compromise 44,000 servers and deploy ransomware. The new issues are CVE-2026-29201, an arbitrary file read in `feature::LOADFEATUREFILE` (CVSS 4.3), CVE-2026-29202, arbitrary Perl code execution via the `create_user` API `plugin` parameter (CVSS 8.8), and CVE-2026-29203, an unsafe symlink / chmod privilege-escalation issue (CVSS 8.8). The article also gives operational guidance: run `/scripts/upcp` or `/scripts/upcp --force`, restart `cpsrvd`, verify the version with `/usr/local/cpanel/cpanel -V`, and review logs back to February 23 for compromise indicators such as `.sorry` files. While the piece is mostly a security news recap rather than original research, it is practically useful for hosting operators because it maps the patching steps and incident-response checks to specific cPanel commands and timestamps.

articles.title: LLMs Corrupt Your Documents When You Delegate
articles.title_display: Study Finds LLM Delegates Corrupt Documents
articles.summary: This paper introduces DELEGATE-52, a benchmark for long delegated workflows across 52 professional domains, including coding, crystallography, and music notation. In a large-scale experiment covering 19 LLMs, the authors report that even frontier models such as Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude 4.6 Opus, and GPT 5.4 corrupted an average of 25% of document content by the end of long workflows. The degradation worsened with larger documents, longer interactions, and distractor files. The authors also tested agentic tool use and found that it did not improve performance on DELEGATE-52. The main takeaway is that current LLMs are not yet reliable delegates for document-heavy work: they can introduce sparse but severe errors that silently accumulate over time, which matters directly for AI-assisted editing, coding agents, and other workflow automation systems.

articles.title: How LEDs are made (2014)
articles.summary: SparkFun’s 2014 factory tour shows the end-to-end LED assembly process at YunSun: die sheets, lead-frame stamping, manual die placement, gold-wire bonding, epoxy molding, curing, lead cutting, and pogo-pin QC testing. The article includes a few concrete details, such as roughly 4,000 dies per sheet for about $12.50, manual alignment at more than 80 dies per minute, a 45-minute bake followed by 8–12 hours of curing, and the observation that the work is done in open air rather than a clean room. It also explains why LED shapes are constrained by mold design and supplier specialization across the tool chain. Useful as an accessible manufacturing walkthrough for hardware engineers, but it is primarily an educational photo essay rather than deep technical analysis.

articles.title: America's carpet capital: an empire and its toxic legacy
articles.title_display: Georgia Carpet Industry’s PFAS Pollution Legacy
articles.summary: AP’s investigative feature details how northwest Georgia’s carpet empire used PFAS-based stain treatments for decades and left a long contamination trail in rivers, wells, soil, dust, and blood. It relies on interviews, court records, internal company notes, EPA history, and blood testing from residents including Dolly Baker, Faye Jackson, Marie Jackson, and former industry worker Lisa Martin. The reporting shows that 3M warned carpet makers as early as 1998–1999 that Scotchgard chemicals were appearing in human blood and persisting in the environment, yet carpet mills continued using related PFAS formulations until 2019 in U.S. production. The story also documents regulatory failures: Dalton Utilities’ wastewater handling, EPA’s slow response, state deference, and the absence of strong federal PFAS controls for years. Measurements cited include Conasauga River samples reportedly more than 30 times proposed EPA drinking-water limits and resident blood levels ranging from 6 ng/mL to 1,336.5 ng/mL. The practical takeaway is that this is a well-sourced environmental accountability investigation, but it is not a software- or engineering-focused piece.

articles.title: All my clients wanted a carousel, now it's an AI chatbot
articles.title_display: Why Clients Keep Asking for Website Chatbots
articles.summary: The post argues that website chatbots have become a status signal rather than a tool, similar to past design fads like carousels, cookie banners, and analytics widgets. The author says clients often want a chatbot because competitors have one, even though they usually dislike using chatbots themselves and find them unhelpful. It contrasts this with fast, minimal “smolweb” sites that users often find easier to read and quicker to use, while noting that simple, well-designed sites are harder to build and easier to dismiss as “not impressive enough.” The piece is a reflective opinion rather than a technical writeup, and its main takeaway is that web design trends are driven by perceived legitimacy as much as usability.

articles.title: The Real Singularity is the Friends We Made Along the Way
articles.title_display: Geohot Argues AI Is Transformation, Not Singularity
articles.summary: This post argues that AI is a major technological shift, but not a singularity or machine-god event. The author frames current hyperscaler GPU spending as a strategic capex race driven by fear of missing out, not proof of imminent superintelligence, and compares the situation to earlier industrial transitions like steam engines and jet engines. It also rejects common doomer claims about the end of capitalism, jobs, or humanity. The piece is mostly a worldview essay rather than an engineering analysis, with no benchmarks, system details, or production evidence. Its practical value is mainly as a contrarian take on AI hype and investment narratives.
